entry.title: AI Runs in the Cloud — So Why Does Opening More Agents Lag Your Computer?
entry.content:
Intro

We had an internal training session at work today, and a coworker asked a couple of questions that really stuck with me:

Is Claude actually running inside my computer? Otherwise how can it find my project, modify files, and even run commands on its own?

Honestly, I think that's a totally fair question. When you watch AI Agent tools like Claude Code, Codex CLI, or Gemini CLI read files, write and edit code, and run tests all by themselves, it really does look like there's an AI living inside your machine.

But the real answer actually splits into two parts. What you see is only partly running on your local machine — the other part runs on a remote server (for example, Claude's models usually run in a remote data center).

So this post is here to clear that up, and I'm aiming it at the total-beginner level, which means I'm not going to start from complicated APIs or JSON.

Once you separate "the model" from "the Coding Agent," the whole thing gets a lot easier to understand.

The model is the brain, the Coding Agent is the hands

When you use Claude Code, there are really three things to keep separate:

  1. Claude's model lives in a remote data center: the model's main job is to understand your request, read what it's given, and decide what to do next.
  2. Claude Code is an app installed on your computer: it's the program that talks to the remote model, manages permissions, and runs tools.
  3. Reading files, editing files, running tests — those really do happen on your computer: the thing actually doing the work is the Claude Code installed on your machine, not the remote model.

If that's still a bit fuzzy, here's an analogy that might help:

  • The remote model is the brain, the local Coding Agent is the hands.
  • The brain figures out the next step; the hands are what actually touch your files and run commands.

So when you install Claude Code, you're not downloading a full Claude model onto your computer — you're installing an app that can talk to the model and use your local tools.

Coding Agents like Codex CLI and Gemini CLI share the same core idea, though each product differs in which tools it can use, where those tools run, and when it needs your permission.

So when you say "fix this bug for me," what actually happens?

Let's set up a scenario. Say you type this into the Claude Code chat window:

Please fix the bug in XXX.js, then run the tests.

Behind the scenes, the flow looks roughly like this:

  1. Claude Code (local) receives your request (the text, a.k.a. the Prompt)
  2. Claude Code sends your request plus whatever info is currently needed to the remote side (Claude's model)
  3. The remote model receives it and decides it should read XXX.js first
  4. The model returns a tool call: "please read XXX.js"
  5. Claude Code (local) checks permissions, then actually reads the file (if it doesn't have permission, it'll pop up and ask you to authorize)
  6. Claude Code hands the file contents back to the remote model
  7. The model reads through the contents, then decides how to change the code and which tests to run

And this whole thing doesn't run just once.

The model reads a file, searches for other code, edits the contents, runs the tests — and if the tests fail, it decides the next step based on the error message.

This loop of "model decides the next step → tool executes → result goes back to the model → model decides the next step again" is called the Agentic Loop.

What is a tool call?

Tool calls are... a little tricky. In short, when you install Claude Code, it ships with a bunch of small programs bundled inside — things like:

  • A file reader (File Reader)
  • A file writer (File Writer)
  • A file deleter (File Deleter)

And so on. So the remote model never reaches directly into your computer — all it does is tell Claude Code:

For my next step, I want to use the "file reader" tool, targeting XXX.js.

Only after Claude Code receives that request does it decide, based on your current permission settings:

  • Is this tool allowed to run?
  • Which folders can it read?
  • Does this operation need to ask the user first?
  • Once it succeeds, what result should go back to the model?

So "the model wants to use a tool" and "the tool actually ran successfully" are two different things.

The model proposes the next step; actually running it is the job of an external program or execution environment.

On top of that, tools don't always run on your computer. Some run on the vendor's servers or in a cloud sandbox — things like Codex cloud tasks, Code Interpreter, and Web Search.

But... why can the remote model see my code?

This is the fun part, and it ties back to the earlier point about "reading files."

The remote model doesn't get to see your entire hard drive, and it doesn't go wandering around your folders on its own.

The reason it can see XXX.js is usually that the local Coding Agent used the file reader tool to grab the contents, then handed the model whatever it needs to finish the task.

Besides file contents, every time the model works on a task it might also receive:

  • The request you just made
  • Earlier conversation history
  • Project rules and operation limits
  • Which tools the model is allowed to use
  • Command output, error messages, and test results

All of this together is what we call the Context.

The model needs to see enough context to know what it did earlier, and what it should do next and how to handle it.

When the conversation gets too long, the Coding Agent might keep only the relevant parts, or summarize and compress the older stuff.

Think of it like this: when your desk can't hold every document at once, you put the unimportant ones away and keep only what the current task needs on top. The technical term for that is Auto Compact.

Even though commands run locally, that doesn't mean none of your data ever leaves your computer. To help the remote model understand your project, the Coding Agent does send file contents, error messages, and tool results. So exactly which data gets sent depends on the product's design, your settings, and the terms of service.

Why do more Agents make your computer lag?

Okay, after all that setup, we can finally talk about the title — because you kind of needed the earlier flow explained before this makes sense.

When a Coding Agent is waiting for the remote model to reply, your computer is just sitting there keeping the app, the display, and the network connection alive. That part doesn't really put any load on your machine.

What actually slows your computer down is multiple Agents doing local work at the same time — things like:

  • Searching or reading a large number of project files simultaneously
  • Running tests, lint, type checks, or full builds at the same time
  • Spinning up multiple dev servers and browsers
  • Installing packages, unzipping files, or building caches
  • Reading and writing lots of files at once, or working across different Git worktrees

Say you have three Agents each running a full test suite at the same time — that's basically no different from you opening three terminals and running the tests yourself.

All that work hits the same local CPU, RAM, and disk, so of course your computer can slow down.

So a more accurate way to put it is:

The more Agents you open, the more local work can run at the same time. The main reason your computer lags is the stacked-up workload — not a remote model reaching through the air to eat your local GPU.

Of course, opening three Agents doesn't automatically make your computer three times slower (imagine if it did — how scary would that be, and how beefy would your machine need to be).

Three Agents all waiting for a remote reply, versus three Agents all building a large project at once, put completely different loads on your computer.

So why does everyone say AI is so GPU- and memory-hungry?

The answer here is really about the model itself — the model needs a ton of compute and memory. But when you're just using a cloud model, what's being consumed is the remote data center's hardware, not your laptop, desktop, or server.

Let me put this a different way:

  • The model weights are like one giant set of reference books: for the model to use what it learned, that data has to be accessible to the system.
  • Memory is like a desk: the bigger the desktop, the more model data, conversation history, and temporary results you can spread out at once.
  • The GPU is like a big crew that can all compute at the same time: the model needs to do a huge amount of math, and a GPU is great for chewing through that repetitive, massive workload together.

So when the model runs, memory mainly holds:

  • The model's own weights
  • The KV Cache needed for the current conversation and input
  • Intermediate results during inference, plus the execution framework

When we say "memory" here, we generally mean CPU memory (RAM) and GPU memory (VRAM), not HDD or SSD storage.

For example, running a 7B model needs roughly 14 GB just for the weights (a rough estimate), and actually running it adds KV Cache and other overhead on top. Quantization can shrink the model, but it can also come with trade-offs in quality, speed, or compatibility.

Large cloud models might even spread their weights across multiple GPUs or multiple machines, so you can't picture it as "the whole model definitely crammed into a single graphics card."

One simple table to see it clearly

Next, here's a table to make the difference between "where the model computes" and "where the tools run" clear:

SituationWhere the model computesWhere the tools runWhat your computer mainly does
Regular cloud chat, no toolsRemote data centerNo extra toolsRender the display, send requests, receive replies
Local Coding Agents like Claude Code, Codex CLIModel usually remoteFile read/write, terminal, etc. often localRun the Agent and tools, using CPU, RAM, disk
Cloud Agents like Codex cloud taskRemote data centerCloud container or sandboxSubmit the task, check results, apply changes
Local models like Ollama, LM StudioYour computerUsually also on your computerEven model inference is on you — can heavily use GPU / unified memory

The most important takeaway from this table is: where the model computes and where the tools run are two separate questions.

The same AI product can offer both local tools and remote tools at once — things like data analysis, Code Interpreter, Web Search, or server-side Code Execution.

So when you see "the AI ran a program for me," you can't immediately assume the program definitely ran on your own computer.

In the end it comes back to which feature you're using, and what that tool's execution environment is.

Local tools really do touch your computer — permissions matter

The model usually lives remotely, and when data is sent over, the model vendor does some basic sensitive-info filtering. But the actions a local Coding Agent runs can genuinely affect your computer.

Because it really can:

  • Modify or create files
  • Delete content
  • Install packages
  • Run tests or start servers
  • Connect to external systems when permission allows

Coding Agents like Claude Code and Codex limit what they can do through permissions, approval rules, or sandboxes. But you need to be clear that not every action is guaranteed to ask you first — it depends entirely on the mode you chose (Auto Mode), the boundaries of your workspace, and the type of operation.

If this is your first time using a Coding Agent, I'd strongly suggest starting with more conservative permissions. When the Coding Agent pops up a permission prompt, check what it's about to read, write, or delete, whether it needs network access, and whether the action is destructive.

If you just mindlessly hit Yes, there's a real chance your privacy and your computer could get leaked or damaged.

Conclusion

Let me wrap up the key points:

  1. Cloud models like Claude and GPT usually do their computation and inference in a remote data center
  2. Claude Code and Codex CLI are Coding Agent apps that run on your local machine
  3. The model only proposes content and next steps; the actual work is done by local, cloud, or third-party tools
  4. Whether opening more Agents causes lag depends on how much local work they're running at the same time
  5. Only when you use a local model does inference directly and heavily use your own GPU / unified memory — though local tools can also use the GPU depending on the work

So, back to the original question: "Is Claude running inside my computer?"

I'm confident you already have a clear answer by now. As long as you look at "where the model computes" and "where the tools run" separately, you won't mix up "AI needs a lot of GPU" with "a Coding Agent slows down my machine" as if they were the same thing.

entry.title: My 100-Day Body Recomposition Experiment, as a Software Engineer
entry.content:
Just a heads-up: this post is a personal experience share, not professional medical advice. Please don't follow it blindly. If you have any concerns, check with a doctor or dietitian first.

Intro

Kind of funny, right? A tech blog bringing up "body recomposition." This was an experiment I started on 2026/3/21, and the goal was to do body recomposition over 100 days — meaning build muscle and drop body fat percentage.

So why did I do this? And along the way, what did it cost me, how did I plan it, how did I execute it, and how did it turn out? This post is here to document and share the process and results of my experiment. If you're an engineer like me and you've been thinking about body recomposition too, maybe this post gives you a tiny bit of direction — and knowing that I've already gone and tested it myself, I'm confident you can do it too.

Why I started this experiment

Let's start with why I suddenly decided to do this.

Through 2024–2025 I was actually already working on one thing: weight control. So I'd built up an exercise habit, and back then what I did was at-home "High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)." Why did I pick that? Honestly the main reason was that I wanted something short, fast, and able to drop weight quickly. xD

But by the end of 2025 (around December), I realized it just wasn't working. My weight had crept all the way up to 82 kg and I'd become what people call a "skinny-fat" guy. Some physical stuff started showing up too — for example, I've always gotten up at 7 a.m. sharp, but it was getting harder and harder to get out of bed, my sleep schedule started drifting, and my skin kept getting worse.

"Skinny-fat" describes someone with thin limbs but a big belly and waist — the whole person looks kind of puffy, like a cream puff, with relatively high body fat.

Because of all that, on 2026/1/1 I started getting ready to do body recomposition. Note that carefully — I only started getting ready.

I went back and reviewed my exercise, diet, sleep, and so on, and after doing my homework for a good while, I didn't officially start body recomposition until 2026/3/21. At that point my weight was 82 kg and my body fat was as high as 28%, and my goal was: "within 100 days, get weight down to the 6x kg range and body fat down to 15–16%."

Before I started, what did I do?

So during 2026/1/1 to 2026/3/21, I mainly did five things:

  • Read a ton of diet-related literature
  • Learned to read food nutrition labels and ingredient lists
  • Made a diet plan
  • Nailed down correct exercise form
  • Learned the body basics (muscle, fat, basal metabolic rate, calories burned, calories consumed, protein intake, carb intake, fat intake)

On top of that, I built an Awesome Gemini Gems (personal assistants built on Gemini) system, poured all this knowledge into Gemini, and made good use of RayFit (my fat-loss companion) to help manage my daily eating.

Then I went to a hospital to get an InBody scan, at least to know some of my own body numbers. At the time they looked like this:

  • Weight: 82 kg
  • Body fat: 28%
  • Obesity degree: 130%
  • Basal metabolic rate: 1597 kcal
  • Visceral fat level: 11

A lot of people honestly couldn't tell I'd gotten this heavy — even I didn't realize it. So before you start body recomposition, I'd really recommend getting a basic InBody scan first, because it helps your future body recomposition a lot. At minimum you need to know your current body state before you can plan the next step.

And a warning here: never, ever blindly chase a "low weight," because low weight doesn't mean you're healthy. What matters more is muscle mass, body fat percentage, obesity degree, basal metabolic rate, visceral fat level, and so on — those are the real indicators of a healthy body. Looking at weight alone is really inaccurate and pointless, because for the human body, a lot of movement and organ protection relies on muscle mass. So whether you have enough muscle is the key to your health.

Reading your InBody numbers

Assuming you've now measured your InBody numbers, you'll see the following:

  • Weight: the total weight of your entire body, including muscle, fat, water, bone, and all other tissues.
  • Skeletal muscle mass: the muscle attached to your bones — the muscles you can control, usually the ones you use when you work out.
  • Body fat mass: the weight of the fat in your body. Fat is how your body stores energy, but too much of it hurts your health.
  • BMI (Body Mass Index): a number used to assess whether your weight is in a healthy range. The formula is: BMI = weight (kg) / height (m) squared.
  • Body fat percentage: the weight of fat in your body as a percentage of your total body weight. This helps you understand whether your body composition is healthy.
  • Visceral fat level: the fat wrapped around your internal organs. Too much visceral fat raises your risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and other chronic conditions.
  • Basal metabolic rate: the minimum energy your body needs to keep you alive at rest. This helps you understand how many calories you need per day to maintain your weight.

These are numbers you absolutely need to understand, because they directly affect your future body recomposition plan — especially body fat percentage, visceral fat level, and basal metabolic rate. Those three have a big impact on both your health and the results of your recomposition.

Some people will see their BMI is normal and just ignore everything else. Honestly, mine was normal too, but BMI isn't accurate. What you really want to watch are the three big numbers I just mentioned — that's the real focus, and it's exactly why people always say BMI isn't accurate.

The basic idea of body recomposition

The basic idea of body recomposition is "build muscle, lower body fat percentage," and these two things support each other.

Why you'd want to "cut fat" is easy to get — that big blob on you is oil, it's fat. "Building muscle" means increasing your body's muscle mass. Besides making you stronger, more muscle also significantly raises your basal metabolic rate, so you burn more calories even at rest. That's one reason some people seem to eat whatever they want and barely gain weight — they've got enough muscle, so their basal metabolic rate is a bit higher (though more often it's because their day-to-day activity level is just naturally high).

Of course, unless you're competing in bodybuilding, you don't need to train to that extreme level. You just need to keep yourself in a state of "enough muscle, low body fat."

Basal metabolic rate?

Next let's get to know the term "basal metabolic rate." Simply put, your basal metabolic rate is the calories your body needs to burn at rest. Even while you sleep, your body burns calories to stay alive — that's your basal metabolic rate (BMR, Basal Metabolic Rate).

But what we rely on each day isn't just BMR — there's also AMR (Active Metabolic Rate), the calories your body needs while you're active. This number is much higher than BMR, because when you're active your body needs to burn more energy to keep moving.

AMR shows up all the time — eating, walking, exercising, all of that falls under AMR. So the calories you need in a day are actually BMR + AMR, and that number is how much you should be eating in a day.

When you want to do body recomposition, the goal is to create a "calorie gap" or "calorie deficit" — the calories you burn in a day need to exceed the calories you eat. That's what makes your body burn its stored fat, which gets you the fat loss.

Basal metabolic rate does have a formula you can use to calculate it, and it's different for men and women, but let's not overcomplicate things here — measuring InBody is faster.

Once you've got all that, next we need to understand another thing: the "calorie gap."

The calorie gap

Calorie gap... calorie gap... so what actually is a calorie gap?

This tripped me up at first too. The reason I made a point of covering the InBody numbers earlier is to introduce this concept of a "calorie gap" or "calorie deficit," because you need to know how many calories you need in a day, then control how many you eat so you actually have a gap.

So how big should the gap be? For example, say you need 2000 calories a day. If you keep your intake at 1500 calories, then you've got a 500-calorie gap, and your body will slowly start burning your stored fat.

And fat isn't purely evil — it's kind of like your body's emergency food reserve. If you've owned a cat you'll get this even better: it's like a cat's primordial pouch.

Think of the human body as a country. That fat is the wealth in the treasury, and when your calorie intake isn't enough, your body dips into those reserves.

Because of this, I really recommend buying a "sports watch" or "fitness band" to track your daily calorie burn. That way you'll know how many calories you need per day, and figuring out your calorie gap gets a lot more accurate.

Body recomposition has something to do with... insulin?

Yep, they're related — because insulin is a really important hormone in your body, and its main job is to regulate blood sugar. After you eat, your blood sugar rises, at which point your pancreas secretes insulin to help your body convert blood sugar into energy, or store the excess as fat.

Any time you eat, you start secreting insulin. Insulin mainly helps glucose enter your cells, and on top of that it also helps "synthesize fat and reduce muscle breakdown." Feels a bit contradictory reading this, right? You want body recomposition, but insulin helps "synthesize fat," so now you're wondering what on earth you're supposed to do.

This is where we bring in another concept: "insulin resistance." When you eat too many carbs for a long time (I'll explain what carbs are later), your pancreas secretes a lot of insulin, and your body starts developing "insulin resistance" — meaning your body's sensitivity to insulin drops. At that point your body starts "storing fat," i.e. you start getting fatter, because your cells have a certain storage limit (a ceiling). Once your cells are packed with energy, the leftover energy naturally gets converted to fat and stored — and that's why you gain weight.

And don't underestimate insulin resistance — it's also the source of a lot of diseases, like diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity, and so on. So you really do need to watch out for it.

Reading this far, I want to add one reminder. When people dig through the research, a lot of them bring up "sugar" and start demonizing it.

But I have to be honest — sugar isn't inherently a bad thing. What matters is how much you eat and what kind of sugar it is, because sugar is one of your body's main energy sources. When you eat too much sugar over a long period, your body starts developing "insulin resistance," at which point it starts "storing fat" — and that's why you gain weight.

Speaking of which, let me fill in a hole I dug earlier: what carbs actually are.

Carbohydrates, put plainly, are nutrients made of three elements — carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. They're the body's most important and most convenient energy source. Picture them as the body's favorite "quick fuel." The white rice, noodles, bread, sweet potatoes, and fruit we normally eat — even the fiber in vegetables — all count as carbs.

So how do sugar and carbs relate? Simple: sugar is just one type of carbohydrate. In other words, "all sugars are carbs, but not all carbs are sugar." Get that idea down first and everything after gets easy.

Carbs can also be split by molecular structure into:

  • Monosaccharides: glucose, fructose, galactose
  • Disaccharides: sucrose, lactose, maltose
  • Polysaccharides: starch, cellulose, glycogen

Monosaccharides and disaccharides are called "simple sugars," while polysaccharides are called "complex carbs." This distinction is based on molecular structure. That's about as far as this little tidbit needs to go — this post isn't here to write chemistry formulas.

Learn to read nutrition labels and ingredients

Next let's talk about "nutrition labels." Before you buy something, especially when you're doing body recomposition, you absolutely need to learn to read nutrition labels, because that's the key to controlling your calorie intake.

Let me use the "Americano" I drink all the time as an example. On most labels you'll basically see the following:

  • Amount per serving
  • N servings per package
  • Calories
  • Protein
  • Fat
    • Saturated fat
    • Trans fat
  • Carbohydrates
    • Sugar
  • Sodium

Of these, there are a few things you most need to watch:

  1. Calories: if you want stricter calorie control, watch this number, because if you don't manage it well you can easily exceed your daily calorie needs and start putting on fat.
  2. Protein: muscle is mainly made of protein.
  3. Fat: watch the type of fat, especially saturated fat and trans fat — both are really bad for your health. What do I mean? Saturated fat raises your risk of cardiovascular disease, and trans fat raises your risk of cardiovascular disease, diabetes, obesity, and other chronic conditions. So try to avoid these two. A lot of products make them hard to avoid, but at least watch the number and try not to overdo it.
  4. Carbohydrates: watch the type of carbs, specifically the "sugar" part. 1 g of sugar is about 0.2 sugar cubes, 5 g = 1 cube, 25 g = 5 cubes (the WHO's recommended daily upper limit for refined sugar for health), 50 g = 10 cubes (the WHO's general daily upper limit for refined sugar). Honestly, if you open any snack in your house and check, you might find it's got way more than 25 g of sugar.
  5. Sodium: basically "salt." Eating too much salt makes you retain water and puffy, because salt makes your body hold on to water. Watch this during body recomposition too, because maybe you normally eat too many high-sodium foods and end up bloated. When you start exercising, the early progress is fast but then it stalls — and the reason for that early speed is that you're "shedding water." So keep an eye on this.

Once you can read these, watch out for one more thing: nutrition labels have a margin of error, roughly ±20%. So don't assume that when it says "Calories: 100 kcal," you're eating exactly 100 kcal — it might actually be somewhere between 80 and 120. But basically, once you start reading nutrition labels, you're already ahead of most people, because most people never look at them — some don't even know what a nutrition label is.

Reading this, some of you might wonder about "refined sugar." It's simple — it's basically "processed sugar," the stuff in candy, desserts, drinks, and so on. Refined sugar spikes your blood sugar fast, which triggers insulin secretion and leads to fat storage, so try to avoid it. "Natural sugar" refers to fruits, vegetables, and the like — natural sugar raises your blood sugar slowly and stimulates insulin less, so relatively speaking, it has a smaller impact on body recomposition.

After all, refined sugar rarely makes you feel full, so your brain keeps signaling you to eat more. Natural sugars, on the other hand, are mostly "whole foods" like fruits and vegetables, and they usually come with fiber that makes your brain feel full — so you don't keep wanting to eat.

Whole foods? Processed foods?

For modern life, we're surrounded by all kinds of "refined" foods. These are foods reshaped through various processing methods. Bread, which everyone eats, is actually a "processed food" too, because it's made by processing wheat flour, adding all sorts of additives, and baking it — and most of these processed foods are "refined foods."

So does that mean you can't eat processed foods? This is a common misconception, because a lot of the food in our lives is processed — milk, yogurt, soy milk, bread, crackers, snacks, and so on are all processed. But processed foods aren't necessarily refined foods, because some are extensions of "whole foods." Milk, yogurt, and soy milk, for example, are made through processing, but they still retain the nutritional character of the original food, so these can be eaten in moderation.

I deliberately used "bread" as an example earlier to show you that what you think of as "processed" isn't always a refined food. What you really need to watch is the "additives," because additives are mostly there to improve texture, extend shelf life, and so on, which can mean a lot of sugar, salt, and oil packed inside. That's what you should really focus on — not every food is evil.

After learning about whole foods and processed foods, a lot of people end up with one question: "So how am I supposed to eat?" It's actually simple — basically just follow these principles:

  • Choose whole foods as much as possible, and avoid refined sugar and highly processed foods.
  • Eat processed foods in moderation, but watch the type and amount of additives.

On top of that, some things are whole foods but their production process has already stripped away their nutritional value. For example, I used to buy fruit drinks. Even ones labeled "100% juice" have had the "fiber" removed during processing for the sake of texture.

Fiber is a carbohydrate your body can't digest or absorb, but it's really important for gut health — it helps your gut move things along, promotes bowel movements, and helps control blood sugar and cholesterol.

If you genuinely can't tell which foods are whole and which are processed, here's a simple rule of thumb:

  • If you can still recognize the food's original form, it's mostly a whole food — apples, bananas, carrots, chicken breast, steak, and so on are all foods whose original form you can directly recognize.

Then pair that with reading the "ingredients" + "nutrition label," and you can roughly tell whether a food is whole or processed.

Because... if a product has a whole pile of ingredients thrown in for no reason, it's probably not a whole food.

Diet planning for body recomposition

I think this part is the hardest for a lot of people. Plenty of exercise material talks about the "calorie gap," but to actually hit that gap you have to weigh your food and calculate calories.

Take white rice — one gram of white rice is about 1.3 kcal, so 100 grams of white rice is...

  • 100 g * 1.3 kcal = 130 kcal

And the calories you need per day — say 2000 kcal — means you have to calculate the total calories of everything you eat in a day and keep it under 2000, so you have a calorie gap.

But... most modern people eat out, so who's realistically going to weigh their food and calculate calories out of nowhere? So here are a few approaches for you:

  1. Prepare your own food:
    • Prepare your own food as much as possible, so you can control the type and amount and avoid overeating calories.
    • For example: cook your own rice, steam vegetables, grill chicken breast, and so on — that way you know exactly what you're eating and how many calories it is.
  2. Choose healthy takeout:
    • If you really don't have time to prepare your own food, then choose healthy takeout and avoid excess calories.
    • For example: pick salad, grilled chicken breast, steamed fish, and avoid fried food, desserts, drinks, and other high-calorie stuff.
  3. Use a food-tracking app:
    • Use a food-tracking app to log everything you eat each day, so you know your daily calorie intake and can keep it within your target range.

For me, I prepare my own food, but I don't specifically weigh it. Instead, I control calories through "portion control" — the "fist rule," where each meal's portion is roughly the size of your fist. That way you can roughly control your daily calorie intake.

One meal roughly controls to:

  • 1 fist of protein (e.g. chicken breast, fish, tofu)
  • 0.5 fist of carbs (e.g. white rice, sweet potato, oats)
  • 2–3 fists of vegetables (e.g. greens, carrots)

This amount gradually increases as you build muscle, because once you have muscle, your body needs more calories to maintain it. If you don't eat enough, your muscle gets broken down instead.

When you start body recomposition, also watch your protein intake. There's basically a formula for it:

  • Protein intake = weight (kg) * 1.6–2.2 g

If you're doing weight training, I'd suggest bumping protein intake up to 2.2 g/kg, which helps you build and maintain muscle without losing it.

Exercise planning for body recomposition

Like I mentioned earlier, through 2024–2025 I was mainly doing at-home "High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)," and the reasons I never slimmed down were these:

  • No proper diet control
  • No understanding of nutrition labels
  • No understanding of the calorie gap or basal metabolism concepts

And the most important one: my body's muscle mass was insufficient. Stepping onto the InBody scan, my left side was in a muscle-deficient state. When your muscle mass is low, no matter how hard you exercise, if your basal metabolic rate doesn't go up AND your diet isn't controlled, it's basically wasted effort.

So the first thing I did in phase one on 2026/3/21 was... "build muscle" — I needed to increase my muscle mass first.

But I didn't go to a gym. I bought some fitness equipment from Decathlon myself:

  • 2, 3, and 5 kg dumbbells, one set each
  • 6 and 8 kg kettlebells, one each
  • A calf-stretch board

Then I paired it with a fitness band to track my daily calorie burn + activity level.

  • March–April was mainly focused on "building muscle"
  • May–June I started adding "cardio"
  • June–July I started ramping up the intensity

Here's the average rep count I did:

  • Bicep training: 150 reps each arm
  • Ab training: 250 reps
  • Back rows: 100 reps
  • Soleus (calf) training: 100 reps
  • Forearm training: 50 reps
  • Shoulder press training: 50 reps
  • High-intensity boxing + running: 30 minutes

(This is basically turning into a One Punch Man special.)

Overall it took at least around two hours.

Each week I'd plan 1–2 "rest days" + "refeed days," because muscle needs time to recover after exercise, and blindly exercising can actually make your body think it's hit a famine or danger and switch to energy-saving mode. Proper refeed days also give your body enough energy to repair muscle, so it actually grows. I'm not telling you to eat extremely clean forever — long-term that's just miserable — so I'll still eat salt-and-pepper chicken, fried chicken, and fried stuff, but I control it to "refeed days" and pick "healthier fried options" like chicken breast, chicken tenders, sweet potato fries, and so on. That way you can enjoy good food without wrecking your body recomposition plan.

On top of that, I also bought a home InBody scale to use at home. The accuracy isn't guaranteed, but at least it gives you a rough sense of whether yesterday's behavior was on track or off.

One important concept here: you can't spot-reduce fat from a specific area — this is where a lot of people get it wrong. Fat loss is usually whole-body, and belly fat is usually the hardest to lose.

Can you do body recomposition without a gym?

Absolutely, but you have to nail correct exercise form first. This post isn't saying gyms are useless or that hiring a trainer is useless — it's that you don't need a gym to do body recomposition, because you can use at-home equipment. But you have to pair it with correct form to actually get the results.

Since I have some athletic background, pairing that with some YouTube workout tutorials let me pick up how to use the muscles pretty quickly. So I can't offer too much advice on this part — everyone's athletic base is different, so you have to find the workout style that fits you and pair it with correct form, otherwise you'll train forever and get nowhere.

Because if you haven't properly learned exercise knowledge, you'll strain something easily, and any strain basically means 1–2 weeks of recovery, which is a net loss. You might as well bite the bullet and pay for a gym and a trainer to teach you correct form — that way you avoid injury and skip a lot of wasted detours.

Plus a gym has lots of equipment you can't use at home — barbells, dumbbells, cable machines, treadmills, spin bikes, and so on. So if you have the time and budget, I'd still suggest going to a gym, because the environment also motivates you to work out more.

For me, it's mostly an introvert thing, so... I'm not used to going to the gym. XD

The workout channels I mostly watch are:

  • 游書庭 - 瘦身有氧
  • 一米六健身服利社
  • shuaisoserious
  • 北木健身治療師 Kopi
  • 健康 2.0
  • 健身工廠

Sharing these here for reference (heads-up: these are Mandarin channels).

What it cost

In the intro I said I'd talk about the cost, so let me be honest with you here.

My costs mainly split into two parts. The first is the one-time equipment investment — the Decathlon dumbbells, kettlebells, and stretch board I mentioned, plus the home InBody scale and the fitness band. These are all things you buy once and use for a long time.

The second is the daily food budget I'm constantly burning through. I originally bought healthy meals out every day, then switched to cooking for myself, which runs about NT$1,000–2,000-something a week. That works out to roughly NT$300 a day, which saves quite a bit compared to buying healthy meals every day — and I eat plenty full.

I don't eat breakfast much, so it's usually just lunch and dinner, because eating breakfast sometimes gives me an upset stomach — at most I'll have coffee.

That said, cooking for yourself has a downside: you have to hit the evening market to buy ingredients, and the whole thing demands more routine and discipline. You have to buy groceries at a set time and prep meals at a set time — you can't just eat whenever the mood strikes like with takeout. But flip it around, and that routine is exactly what body recomposition needs most, so for me it's actually a plus.

The process and results

So, earlier my starting numbers were:

  • Weight: 82 kg
  • Body fat: 28%
  • Obesity degree: 130%
  • Basal metabolic rate: 1597 kcal
  • Visceral fat level: 11

100 days later, on 2026/7/4, my home InBody numbers were:

  • Weight: 67.9 kg
  • Body fat: 16.5%
  • Obesity degree: 97%
  • Basal metabolic rate: 1635 kcal
  • Visceral fat level: 4

Of course, a home InBody isn't as accurate as a hospital machine, but let me be clear about one thing: weight is measured directly, so that number is accurate — and obesity degree, which is derived from weight, isn't far off either. What actually has error is the stuff "estimated" via bioelectrical impedance, like body fat percentage, visceral fat level, and basal metabolic rate. From comparing hospital and home machines myself, the gap is roughly 4–5%, and home machines usually estimate a bit optimistically, so I conservatively put my body fat somewhere around 17–18%.

Also, at the start my InBody showed my left side as muscle-deficient, but both sides have recovered to normal now.

Overall though, I lost 14.1 kg, my body fat dropped by more than 10 percentage points even using conservative numbers, my obesity degree went from 130% to 97%, my visceral fat level dropped from 11 to 4, and my basal metabolic rate ticked up slightly too.

Losing weight this fast — didn't something go wrong with your body? The answer is... no. I got blood tests done throughout this period and the results were all normal. My blood pressure, blood sugar, cholesterol, and so on are all healthier than before.

Another change I really felt is my sleep schedule. Like I mentioned, I used to have a harder and harder time getting up at 7. Now I not only get up at 7 no problem, the first thing I do after getting up is take a cold shower.

But let me be honest about two things:

  • First, averaging close to 1 kg lost per week is actually on the fast side. I only dared do it because I paired it with blood tests and data monitoring. I don't recommend copying this pace directly — going slower is actually safer and easier to sustain.
  • Second, under a calorie gap this big, honestly "hanging on to as much muscle as possible" is already pretty good. So strictly speaking, what I accomplished in these 100 days is "lots of fat loss, holding on to muscle as best I could." And that's exactly what most people with high body fat should do in phase one anyway — only after your body fat comes down do you really focus on stacking muscle up.

At work I still eat takeout normally and order lunch boxes with coworkers, but I try to pick healthier contents — chicken breast, fish, and so on — and avoid fried food, desserts, drinks, and other high-calorie stuff. I'll peel the skin off braised chicken legs, and I stick to sugar-free drinks.

Conclusion

Through the whole process, I personally think the most important thing is treating it as a "long-term battle." Body recomposition isn't something you finish in the short term — it takes long-term effort and persistence, especially in diet control and building an exercise habit, which both take long-term learning and adjusting.

And you also have to "deliberately" schedule certain things in. If you had zero exercise habit before, you have to deliberately carve out workout time and build the habit, so you can keep it going.

The process is honestly painful. A big reason I bought a home InBody scale was to let myself see that today's me is better than yesterday's, paired with Gemini Gems to give me some advice and direction (and even encouragement, lol).

So this post is about wrapping up here. I hope it helps those of you wanting to do body recomposition, because it's a long-term battle — there's no shortcut, only continued effort and persistence. And for now I'm still maintaining my physique, because I really don't want to gain it all back.

One last time, here's the Awesome Gemini Gems I'm using — it's got plenty of Gems I've prepared, and you can open them up and use them directly.

entry.title: New Claude Code Hijacks Mouse Clicks in Your Terminal? Kill It with CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS
entry.content:
Claude Code now captures mouse events inside the terminal to give you click-to-expand, hover, scroll acceleration, and so on — but that also means you can't select text or click links with the mouse anymore. To turn it off, there's no settings.json field and no CLI flag — only environment variables. The one I reach for the most is CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 (v2.1.195+), which kills clicks / drag / hover but keeps the scroll wheel.

Intro

After the recent Claude Code update, the terminal now supports clicking things inside it — Yes, No, that kind of stuff. The problem is, as a developer I usually have multiple projects open, and every time I switch back to a project I end up clicking Yes by accident — when I really didn't want to run the thing!

The whole experience is pretty rough, so I figured I'd document how to fix it.

Why is my mouse suddenly getting hijacked?

Basically, in v2.1.195 Claude Code added mouse interactions inside the terminal — drag, click, hover, all of it. Previous versions didn't have this.

The feature itself is fine I think, but the thing is, as a developer I'm constantly bouncing between N projects, and I live inside the VS Code integrated terminal. So every time I switch back to a project, my mouse click lands on YES by accident and runs something I didn't want to run. Pretty bad experience.

And imagine Claude Code asks me whether to run:

rm -rf ...

and I accidentally click YES, and now stuff is gone that I didn't want gone. That's actually kind of serious.

The behavior is controlled by the environment variable CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS. This is the "kill click / drag / hover, keep the scroll wheel" version — for me personally this is enough.

CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 # Disable mouse click/drag/hover, keep scrolling

CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=0 # Enable mouse click/drag/hover

If you don't even want the scroll wheel hijacked, use the other one instead:

CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=1 # Disable mouse capture entirely (scrolling included)

CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE=0 # Enable mouse capture

There's currently no /config menu or settings.json field for this, so environment variables are the only way.

Adding it to .zshrc

I'm on zsh so I'll only walk through zsh here, but if you're on bash or fish it's basically the same idea.

Open ~/.zshrc and add this at the bottom:

# Disable Claude Code mouse click/drag capture (keep scrolling)
export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1

Save it, then either:

# Open a new terminal window
# Or run in your current window:
source ~/.zshrc

An already-running claude session won't pick this up automatically — you have to quit and restart Claude Code for the new env var to take effect.

bash users edit ~/.bashrc instead, fish users add set -gx CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS 1 to ~/.config/fish/config.fish — same idea.

Don't want to touch config? Three bypass hotkeys

If you don't want to edit .zshrc and you just need to select some text occasionally, every terminal has a one-off bypass key for the mouse capture:

TerminalHotkey
iTerm2Hold Option while dragging
macOS TerminalHold Fn while dragging
VS Code / Cursor integrated terminalHold Shift while dragging
Other terminalsUsually Shift while dragging too

VS Code also has a setting terminal.integrated.macOptionClickForcesSelection: true, which makes Option + click directly force the cursor position — that's a VS Code-specific setting, nothing to do with Claude Code itself.

Wrap up

Adding mouse interaction to Claude Code is a nice idea in theory, but for people who constantly switch projects and like selecting text with the mouse, it's honestly pretty annoying.

The simplest fix is to drop CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_MOUSE_CLICKS=1 into your ~/.zshrc. Scroll wheel stays, clicks and drags stop getting hijacked, and you stop accidentally hitting YES and firing off commands you didn't want.

entry.title: design.md: If Your AI-Generated UI Keeps Drifting, You're Missing Google's design.md
entry.content:
design.md is a format spec released by Google Labs that splits a design system into two layers — "YAML design Tokens" and "Markdown design rationale" — so that AI Coding tools like Claude Code and Cursor see more than just hex codes and font sizes; they actually understand the design intent behind them. The built-in CLI handles lint (including WCAG contrast checks), diff, and export to Tailwind or W3C DTCG. It's currently in alpha.

Intro

Have you ever done some Vibe Coding and noticed the AI's layout is always a little off, and no matter how many times you say "the spacing is wrong here, the colour is inconsistent there", it still produces a slightly broken screen? If yes, it's because you haven't given the AI the rules of your design system properly.

To solve exactly this, this post walks through design.md, an AI design system spec released by Google Labs. The goal is to let AI Coding tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and GitHub Copilot actually read your design system and produce consistent UI, so you don't have to keep repeating "wrong colour, wrong font size" over and over.

If you're not familiar yet with rules files for AI like CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md, take a look at CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI: Writing Tips That Make AI Understand Your Project and Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP first — it'll make a lot more sense why design.md exists.

Where design.md came from: incubated from Stitch

As always, let's do a quick history detour first.

design.md actually comes out of Stitch, Google's AI design tool (first shown at Google I/O 2025). Stitch is a UI design tool powered by Google Gemini — you give it a text Prompt or upload a sketch, and it quickly generates a UI screen (frontend code included).

So when it launched, it was honestly pretty wild.

Then somewhere around 2026, Google released the design.md format itself under Apache 2.0 on GitHub (google-labs-code/design.md).

What does that mean? It basically means they're trying to "standardise" Stitch's design system rules.

After all, the most common pain point for Vibe Coding developers is inconsistent UI and inconsistent style. But if you open-source this design spec, the business value gets pretty huge — whether you use Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, or any other AI Coding tool, you can use this spec to produce consistent UI.

Wait, you're wondering what business value open-sourcing brings?

A few examples:

  • Becoming the industry standard means owning the definition. Think of it like OpenAPI or Kubernetes back in the day — when they were first open-sourced they looked harmless, but once everyone started using them, Google's voice became the loudest about where the spec should go, what fields to add, what Token types to support. Because they're the one in charge.
  • Other AI tools voluntarily become allies. Once Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot start marketing "we support design.md" as a selling point, those competitor tools are basically promoting Google's format for free. Google doesn't even need to spend marketing budget — super cheap.
  • The training data flywheel. Once it's open-sourced, people will put their own project's design.md in public GitHub repos. Google can then use those files to train "AI that generates UI" — structured design Tokens plus human-written design rationale is honestly the perfect combo for AI training data. Future Gemini gets to use this public data to become stronger and get a better excuse to charge more per Token.
  • Stitch's free marketing funnel. Once developers get used to design.md, when they need a "design tool that speaks design.md", the first thing they think of is Stitch — because Stitch is the source.

So when big companies open-source things and it looks like they're losing money, they're often trading time for ecosystem. Chromium, Kubernetes, TensorFlow all played this game — on the surface it's a community contribution, but in reality it's using "free" to lock in the standard early.

What problem is design.md actually solving?

So... what problem does design.md actually solve? Basically just this:

Making sure the UI that AI generates uses the same colours, font sizes, and corner radii every time.

This is really, really important. I'm sure everyone Vibe Coding has run into this — you ask the AI to make a Button, and then...

  • The first time it gives you #3B82F6
  • The second time #2563EB
  • The third time straight up bg-blue-500

The screen looks fine, all blue. But they're not actually the same blue, so your project ends up with a whole watercolour palette of "blues". Font sizes have the same issue — sometimes 1.25rem, sometimes 20px, sometimes text-xl.

This kind of inconsistency makes the UI feel disjointed, which makes the user experience inconsistent, which hurts the brand.

So why does this happen? The main reason is you haven't given the AI a "design system rules" document.

Someone might say: "Can't I just put it in CLAUDE.md?"

You can. But you have to know that CLAUDE.md (and AGENTS.md) are "natural-language rules" for the AI — there's no structured colour palette or Token system. After reading, the AI still has to guess "which hex is Primary" or "which corner radius should this button use", and it ends up guessing in the end anyway.

So design.md is basically turning that "design system rules" document into a file that machines can parse precisely + humans can understand the reasoning behind.

So what does design.md actually look like?

We've kept saying "machines can parse precisely + humans can understand the reasoning behind", so what does this file actually look like? Pretty simple — design.md is just a Markdown file.

The structure splits into two parts:

  • YAML Front Matter: the precise design Tokens (colours, typography, spacing, corner radius, components).
  • Markdown body: the design rationale — why this colour, when to use it, when not to use it.

By splitting the file this way, the AI doesn't just know "Primary is #1A1C1E" — it also knows "Primary is the deep ink used for headlines and core text". So when it produces UI, it's a lot less likely to use the wrong colour for a button or the wrong size for a piece of text.

YAML Token block

The YAML block looks roughly like this:

---
name: Heritage
colors:
  primary: "#1A1C1E"
  secondary: "#6C7278"
  tertiary: "#B8422E"
  neutral: "#F7F5F2"
typography:
  h1:
    fontFamily: Public Sans
    fontSize: 3rem
  body-md:
    fontFamily: Public Sans
    fontSize: 1rem
rounded:
  sm: 4px
  md: 8px
spacing:
  sm: 8px
  md: 16px
components:
  button-primary:
    backgroundColor: "{colors.tertiary}"
    textColor: "{colors.on-tertiary}"
    rounded: "{rounded.sm}"
---

A few key points:

  • colors, typography, rounded, spacing, components are the five main Token categories.
  • Tokens can reference each other — for example, button-primary's backgroundColor is written as "{colors.tertiary}", so when you change the tertiary colour later, everything using it updates automatically.
  • Colours support any valid CSS colour string: hex, rgb, hsl, oklch — all good.

If you've used Tailwind's tailwind.config.js or the W3C Design Tokens Format Module (DTCG), this'll feel really familiar.

Markdown body block

After the closing --- of the YAML, you get the design reasoning written for humans (and AI) to read.

Here's the officially recommended section order:

  • Overview
  • Colors
  • Typography
  • Layout
  • Elevation & Depth
  • Shapes
  • Components
  • Do's and Don'ts

Only Colors (and the primary colour inside it) is required. The other sections are optional — write them only if your design system has that piece.

A concrete example — the Colors block might look like this:

## Colors

Primary (#1A1C1E): Deep ink for headlines and core text.
Use on light surfaces to maximize legibility.

Tertiary (#B8422E): Warm rust accent reserved for primary actions
and brand moments. Avoid using as body text colour due to contrast.

This is basically telling the AI "this colour is called Tertiary, use it for primary action buttons, don't use it for body text".

Wait — there's a CLI!?

Yep, design.md also ships with some really handy tools. Mostly they do four things:

  • lint
  • diff
  • export
  • spec

Installation and usage is super simple:

npm install @google/design.md
npx @google/design.md lint DESIGN.md

ps. If you're on Windows, the .md extension might get eaten by file association, so you'll need the designmd alias:

npx -p @google/design.md designmd lint DESIGN.md

So let's go through what each tool does.

lint: validate the DESIGN.md structure

I'm pretty sure lint will feel familiar to any developer — it's the same concept as ESLint, Stylelint, Prettier.

In short, lint checks the following:

  • Whether the YAML schema is correct
  • Whether Token references are broken (e.g. you wrote {colors.tertiery}, missing an a?!)
  • Whether there's a primary colour
  • Whether WCAG AA contrast passes (I think this one's the killer feature)
  • Whether there are orphan Tokens that are defined but never used
  • Whether the section order matches the recommendation

For the WCAG AA rule, it'll straight up tell you "on-secondary against secondary only hits X.X:1 contrast, doesn't pass AA". So accessibility checks are baked right into the design system file.

diff: compare two versions

This one is honestly pretty fun — just like git diff, you can compare two versions of DESIGN.md. Usage is simple:

npx @google/design.md diff old/DESIGN.md new/DESIGN.md

It'll tell you which Tokens changed, which were deleted, which were added.

The main use case: when you're doing a version bump on your design system, swapping primary from #1A1C1E to #0A0A0A, and you want to know "how many components in the project are going to change colour because of this" — diff lists every affected Token so you can quickly see what needs updating.

export: convert to other formats

The export tool is genuinely impressive in my opinion, especially if you want to treat design.md as the "upstream" source and convert it into Tailwind / CSS / DTCG formats that the frontend actually uses.

FormatDescription
json-tailwindConverts to a Tailwind v3 config object
css-tailwindConverts to a Tailwind v4 @theme CSS block
dtcgConverts to the W3C Design Tokens Format Module

Usage:

npx @google/design.md export DESIGN.md --format json-tailwind > tailwind.config.js
npx @google/design.md export DESIGN.md --format css-tailwind > tailwind.css
npx @google/design.md export DESIGN.md --format dtcg > design-tokens.json

The upside here is you only have to focus on design.md, then export to Tailwind / CSS / DTCG. Frontend, designers, and AI Coding tools all use the same source of truth.

(This one's seriously powerful.)

spec: hand the spec to the AI

Last one is spec, also dead simple to use:

npx @google/design.md spec

This command outputs design.md's own format spec.

After that, you can hand the spec to your AI Coding tool of choice (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot) and tell it "please follow this spec when reading my DESIGN.md". The AI then knows how to parse the YAML schema, that Token references use {path.to.token} syntax, what's required, what's optional.

Add --rules and it'll output the linting rules as well.

So how do you get a DESIGN.md in the first place?

In case you're wondering where DESIGN.md actually comes from, there are basically two ways:

  1. Write one by hand, following the YAML + Markdown structure above.
  2. Pick a style you like from VoltAgent/awesome-design-md, drop its DESIGN.md straight into your project root, then tell the AI:

Please generate UI following the spec in DESIGN.md

That's it. Pretty simple, right?

How do you use it with AI Coding tools?

Now that we've covered design.md's structure and CLI tools, let's talk about how to use it with AI Coding tools.

Since design.md's whole design goal is "turn the UI design system into something that can be continuously understood by AI", the usage is pretty simple:

  1. Put DESIGN.md in your project root.
  2. In CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / .cursor/rules/*.mdc, add a line: "For UI-related tasks, please read DESIGN.md first and strictly follow the Tokens and design rationale inside."

Yep, that's it. Super easy.

One small reminder at the end, though, and it's kind of an old chestnut at this point:

Don't stuff design.md with irrelevant junk.

The whole design philosophy is to give the AI a "distilled" version of your design knowledge — same direction as Agent Skills that I shared before. Give the AI the key points, not everything.

Right now, design.md doesn't ship with official integration examples for Claude Code / Cursor / Copilot — only the CLI and TypeScript API. So you need to guide the AI to read design.md via your CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / rules file.

Wrap-up

Let me wrap this up.

So what exactly is design.md?

  • A design system document for AI Coding tools, in a "YAML Token + Markdown design rationale" format.
  • The pain point it solves is AI-generated UI having inconsistent colours, font sizes, and spacing.
  • The built-in CLI can lint (with WCAG contrast checks), diff, export to Tailwind / DTCG, and output a spec for the AI.

Hope this post helps you fix the "AI generates UI with inconsistent colours, font sizes, and spacing" pain so your AI Coding workflow goes a lot more smoothly.

entry.title: Stats — The Free Open-Source macOS Menu Bar System Monitor I Use Every Day (CPU, GPU, Temperature, Network)
entry.content:
Intro

This post is about my favourite tool on macOS — Stats. I recently jumped from an M1 to an M5, and even though Apple's migration tool moved everything over cleanly, Stats somehow refused to migrate. So it ended up being basically the first app I reinstalled on the new machine, which is a pretty good summary of how much I rely on it. This post covers what it does, how to install it, and a few of my own setup habits.

If you've read my earlier posts — How Should You Install Software on a Mac? Homebrew vs Official Download vs curl or Raycast Extensions I Actually Use — this one belongs in the same series. All of them are tools I've kept on every Mac for years.

So what is Stats?

Quick intro first.

Stats is a system monitor for macOS. Compared to Activity Monitor, it puts everything you'd want to glance at right where you can see it — I check Mac temperatures, network throughput, and memory use throughout the day without ever opening anything.

It's an MIT-licensed open-source project, so it's completely free, no feature gates, no ads, no upgrade nags. And you don't really need to worry about it doing anything sketchy in the background — if it did, you wouldn't be the only one to find out, since a lot of people use it.

Here's the quick rundown of what it can monitor:

ModuleWhat it shows
CPUProcessor usage, per-core state, frequency
GPUGPU usage (Apple Silicon and external GPU both supported)
MemoryRAM usage, swap state
DiskPer-disk read/write speed, remaining space
SensorsSensor readings (CPU temp, GPU temp, voltage, power, etc.)
NetworkLive up/down throughput, public IP, Wi-Fi signal strength
BatteryBattery level, health, charge/discharge state
BluetoothBattery level of connected Bluetooth devices
ClockMultiple timezone clocks
RemoteRemote monitoring of other Macs

Pretty cool, right? Stats also has a Remote module. If you've got a Mac mini at home running OpenClaw or Hermes, this is genuinely useful — you can keep an eye on the remote machine straight from the menu bar instead of opening a remote desktop session.

I haven't actually used Remote myself yet, but reading through the Stats site writeup it looks pretty nice.

Installing Stats

Enough preamble — how do you install it? My recommendation is still Homebrew.

A single command in the terminal:

brew install --cask stats

Stats is a GUI application, so you have to use --cask. brew install stats without --cask will fail, since Homebrew will go looking for a command-line formula by that name.

If you'd rather skip Homebrew, head to the GitHub Releases page and grab the latest Stats.dmg directly.

The modules I actually use

Going through each module one by one.

CPU module

CPU is one I leave on permanently. Being able to see live CPU usage means I can answer "what is this thing doing right now?" almost instantly — especially when the Mac suddenly feels sluggish. The first thing I want to know is whether the CPU is pinned.

GPU module

I turn this one on when I'm running an LLM locally. GPU usage spikes hard in that scenario, so a quick glance tells me whether the model is actually doing work or stuck somewhere mid-step.

Memory module

I watch memory closely too — once you run out, the system starts swapping to disk and everything slows to a crawl.

One thing to mention — macOS handles memory differently from Windows. It'll happily use unused RAM as cache, so you might see Memory used looking high while the system actually has plenty of free room.

Disk module

I rarely look at this one, so I usually keep it off — unless I'm doing something disk-heavy and want to see throughput.

Sensors module

The Sensors module is genuinely important to me — I'm mostly on an Air, and if I don't watch temperatures the Mac will hit thermal throttling and everything stutters.

Stats reads temperatures straight into the menu bar:

  • Per-core CPU temperatures
  • GPU temperature
  • SSD temperature
  • Battery temperature
  • Various motherboard zones
  • Overall power draw

One thing worth noting — for this module the temperatures basically only show up in the menu bar.

Network module

Another one I leave on permanently. Whether the network's working — and how fast it's running — is one of the things I most want at a glance.

Stats fetches your public IP by calling an external API. If that bothers you privacy-wise, you can disable it in the Network module settings.

Battery module

Hmm, this one's pretty useful if you're on an Intel-era Mac — Intel Macs have noticeably less stable battery behaviour and health compared to Apple Silicon, so I'd recommend keeping this one on if that's you.

Bluetooth module

I barely touch this one. If your Bluetooth feels flaky, it's worth opening up.

Clock module

I don't really use this one either, but the convenience is that you can pin multiple timezones — handy if you're collaborating across time zones (probably).

At most I pin a US West Coast time, mainly so I can check maintenance windows on overseas services.

Extra tip — desktop widgets

The menu bar gets crowded fast, so I move a few of the things I care about onto the desktop instead. Right-click the desktop → Edit Widgets:

Then find the widget you want and drag it onto the desktop:

This lets you keep more detail on the desktop itself — for me, it's CPU, GPU, and memory usage.

Common questions

A couple of issues that come up.

Desktop widgets show "No data available"?

You need to enable the "macOS widgets" option for them to work. Stats's desktop widgets are built on top of macOS's native Widget framework, so if that option's off, you'll just see "No data available" forever.

Heads-up though — enabling this also makes Stats hammer disk writes pretty constantly. The maintainer's tracking the issue: Widgets do not update or high disk writes.

Desktop widgets stuck on "Enable in Settings"?

This one seems to happen regularly with no obvious workaround — I'm just waiting on an upstream fix.

Wrap-up

Quick wrap-up.

The reasons I personally recommend Stats:

  • Open source, free, MIT-licensed
  • Modular — turn on what you want, leave the rest off
  • Clean interface, with localisation
  • Surfaces things macOS itself isn't very forthcoming about, like CPU temperature

Hope this is useful.

entry.title: Make /llms.txt Return text/markdown on GitHub Pages: A Cloudflare Transform Rule Guide
entry.content:
Intro

I've written before about Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO and From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready's AI Agent Readiness Test. Both touch on /llms.txt — basically an index file that tells AI search engines what good stuff your site has. And one of the key things is that you really need to serve it with Content-Type: text/markdown.

So I went ahead and dropped /llms.txt and /llms-full.txt onto my gh-pages site like a good boy, ran curl -I to double-check, and what came back was...

HTTP/2 200
content-type: text/plain

So this post is about how I used a Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule to flip that content-type: text/plain into text/markdown; charset=utf-8. No Worker required, the free plan covers it.

Why is /llms.txt coming back as text/plain?

The answer is pretty boring: because the file extension is .txt (yep, that's literally it).

Every web server, when it serves a static file, looks up a table called the MIME type map to decide what Content-Type header to send. GitHub Pages uses mime-db, which is the industry-standard mapping table. The relevant entries look like this:

ExtensionMapped Content-Type
.txttext/plain
.mdtext/markdown
.htmltext/html
.xmlapplication/xml

So anything ending in .txt gets tagged as text/plain — even if you wrote markdown inside it, the web server doesn't peek at the body. It only looks at the extension.

You might be wondering at this point: AI can still read text/plain just fine, right? The content is markdown either way, so what's the big deal?

True — AI can definitely still read it. But there are two small problems:

  1. You lose the explicit format signal: when an AI crawler gets text/plain, it treats the content as plain text and won't necessarily kick in its markdown parsing logic. Headings, lists, links — all that structure can end up being treated as flat prose, and you lose the actual benefit of writing in markdown.
  2. Some tools key off Content-Type: Anthropic and OpenAI both mention in their docs that sites serving markdown should label it as text/markdown. That way an AI Agent immediately knows "ah, this is clean markdown intended for me" and treats it accordingly.

So getting Content-Type right matters more than it sounds.

Why a Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule?

For a need this simple — "I just want to override Content-Type" — the Response Header Transform Rule is the cleanest tool for the job. On top of that, my domain is already on Cloudflare anyway (and gh-pages itself doesn't give you anywhere to touch response headers).

So let me walk through how to set it up.

The setup

The whole thing takes maybe a few minutes.

Step 1: Get to the Transform Rules page

  1. Log into the Cloudflare Dashboard
  2. Pick the domain you want to set this up on (I'm using my blog israynotarray.com as the example)
  3. In the left sidebar, click Rules → Transform Rules
  4. Find the Response Header Transform Rules section and click Create rule on the right

Here you pick "Response Header Transform Rules":

Step 2: Name the rule

I named mine Set markdown content-type so I can tell at a glance later what this rule does.

You could also call it llms.txt markdown header or whatever you prefer.

Step 3: Write the match condition

This is the part where things get a little tricky lol.

So my personal recommendation: just use the Edit expression mode and paste the whole thing in:

(http.request.uri.path eq "/llms.txt") or (http.request.uri.path eq "/llms-full.txt") or (ends_with(http.request.uri.path, ".md"))

This expression does three things:

  • Matches /llms.txt
  • Matches /llms-full.txt
  • Matches anything ending in .md (if you've set up a "one .md per article" pattern)

After you hit Done, your screen should look like this:

Step 4: Set the header to inject

This is the most important step. Scroll down to the Then... section, click the Set a new header button, and three fields will expand for you to fill in:

  • Action: pick "Set static"
  • Header name: Content-Type
  • Value: text/markdown; charset=utf-8

I'd recommend keeping the charset=utf-8 part — without it, Chinese (or any non-ASCII) content can end up garbled on some clients.

Leave Place at on the default "Last", hit Deploy on the bottom right, and you're done.

Step 5: Verify with curl

Once deployed, open your terminal and run curl -I to inspect the response headers:

curl -I https://your-domain/llms.txt

You should see something like:

HTTP/2 200
content-type: text/markdown; charset=utf-8
server: cloudflare

If content-type is now text/markdown; charset=utf-8, the rule is live.

If you're still seeing text/plain, it's probably Cloudflare's edge cache hanging onto the old version. Either add -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" and re-curl, or go to the Caching page in the Cloudflare Dashboard and hit "Purge cache".

Wrapping up

A few takeaways:

  1. On gh-pages, anything ending in .txt defaults to text/plain. When that's your /llms.txt, AI crawlers don't really treat it as markdown.
  2. A Cloudflare Response Header Transform Rule fixes /llms.txt, /llms-full.txt, and any .md files all at once — and the free plan's 10-rule allowance is more than enough.
  3. When writing the match condition, go with Edit expression mode — it skips the easy mistake of picking "Full URI" instead of "URI Path" in the dropdown.
  4. After deploying, curl -I is your friend for confirming the header rewrite actually took effect.

Once this rule's in place, the next AI crawler that fetches /llms.txt will get a clear markdown signal.

If you want to go further and do actual Markdown Negotiation (where the AI sends Accept: text/markdown and you transform the entire HTML article into markdown on the fly), you'll need to step up to a Cloudflare Worker — I covered the full Worker code for that in From 3 to 100: Passing isitagentready's AI Agent Readiness Test.

With this in place, the final piece of the AEO setup falls into place. Hope this post gives you a clearer picture of what Cloudflare Transform Rules can do.
